lesson.menu_desc: Holding a real conversation & asking indirectly -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 -- The Confident Conversationalist
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 11 -- The Confident Conversationalist

reading.rtitle: The Lost Art of Talking to Strangers
paras: Most of us imagine that good conversationalists are simply born that way -- naturally charming people who can strike up a conversation with anyone. In reality, the skill is far more learnable than it looks. The people who put others at ease are not the ones who talk the most; they are the ones who notice the most. They read the room, they ask, and then they genuinely listen.

A surprising amount of it comes down to softening. A blunt 'Where are you from?' can feel like an interrogation, whereas 'I was wondering where you were based' invites someone in. The same question, asked indirectly, comes across as warmer and more tactful. Skilled talkers also rescue a lull instead of fearing it: a small comment, a follow-up question, and the conversation keeps going.

Perhaps the most freeing idea is that you do not need to be witty to build rapport. You need curiosity and a little courage. Ask one more question than feels comfortable, resist the urge to change the subject too soon, and you will find that most strangers are quietly delighted that someone bothered to ask.
reading.source: Adapted from a feature on social skills, The Connection Review

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point?
choices: Good conversationalists are born, not made, so the skill cannot be learned.
Being a good conversationalist is a learnable skill built on noticing, asking and listening -- not on talking the most.
The best way to connect with strangers is to be as witty and entertaining as possible.

items: According to the writer, good conversationalists are mainly the ones who talk the most.
They are the ones who notice the most, ask, and listen.
Asking a question indirectly can make you come across as warmer and more tactful.
'I was wondering where you were based' invites someone in, unlike a blunt question.
The writer says skilled talkers are afraid of a lull and avoid it at all costs.
They rescue a lull with a small comment or a follow-up question instead of fearing it.
The writer believes you need to be witty to build rapport.
You need curiosity and a little courage, not wit.

matching.title: Match the direct question to its softer, indirect version
words: What time does the tour start?
defs: Do you happen to know if this seat is free?
Could you tell me where the station is?
I was wondering whether you live around here.
Would you mind telling me how much your ticket was?
Do you know what time the tour starts?

lf.title: From blunt to tactful -- notice the register

items: You are at a hotel breakfast and want to know if the museum nearby is open today. Ask the guest next to you -- indirectly and politely.
A lull falls in your chat with a fellow traveller. Rescue it: make a small comment and ask one follow-up question to keep the conversation going.
You'd like to know where a stranger is from, but a blunt question feels too direct. Ask in a way that puts them at ease.
You want to make a good impression on a new acquaintance at a dinner. Strike up a conversation and find one thing you have in common.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each direct question as an indirect one
items: What time does the museum close?
How do I get to the old town?
Does this bus stop at the station?
Do you happen to know if

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: Could you tell me where the nearest pharmacy is?
Do you know what time the museum closes?
I was wondering whether breakfast is included.
Would you mind telling me how I get to the old town?
Do you happen to know if this bus stops at the station?
answerkey.note: Form check: indirect question = opener (Could you tell me / Do you know / I was wondering whether...) + STATEMENT word order. No inversion, no do/does in the second part; use if/whether for yes/no questions.

listenings.text: Sorry to bother you -- I was wondering if you could help me out. I'm new in town and a little lost. Do you happen to know whether the old market is open on a Sunday? And would you mind telling me which bus goes up to the cathedral? I don't want to take the wrong one. You seem to know the area well. I hope you don't mind me asking -- are you a local, or just visiting like me? It's a lovely city, isn't it? Anyway, thank you so much. It's so much nicer to ask a real person than to stare at a map.

For most of my life, I was the quiet one at the dinner table. I used to think you had to be funny or clever to be good at conversation. I was wrong. Over the years, I learned a simple trick: I stopped worrying about being interesting and started being interested. Now, when I meet someone new, I ask one gentle question, and then another. I let a pause sit instead of panicking. I try to read the room. And almost every time, that small bit of curiosity turns a stranger into a real conversation. You don't need the perfect words. You just need to be genuinely curious about the person in front of you.
